title: FHS final — The Other Wes Moore ecological-model essay (1/12/21)
html:
TL;DR

  • UO FHS (Family & Human Services) final project — applies Bronfenbrenner's ecological model (micro/meso/exo/macro) to The Other Wes Moore.
  • Issue Yahir picks: accessibility of higher education for poor Black/brown communities — "underfunded schools vs. wealthy public schools" + the school to prison pipeline.
  • Compares Author Wes (military school → Rhodes Scholar → Johns Hopkins) vs. Other Wes (drug-dealing → life sentence) through their family microsystems.
  • Notes redlining → segregated underfunded schools as the macrosystem driver; Job Corps and scholarships flagged as the structural escape valves.
  • Reads as Yahir's first-pass academic articulation of his own "first to attend university" status — same energy as the first gen guilt poem but in coursework register.

Notes

This is a UO online-summer-2020 coursework artifact (canvas.uoregon.edu links in the header). The essay covers all four ecological levels with novel-citation pairs: father absence + alcoholism in Bernard's case (micro), the mom/grandparent collaboration vs. mom-alone divergence (meso), military school + Rhodes vs. job corps (exo), institutional racism + media representation (macro). Yahir credits "persistence, positivity, support" and "choosing good peers" as the takeaway — the same vocabulary that appears in his Why OSU essay and his later first-gen-identity writing. Worth flagging: the analytical frame here (ecological systems on a family) is the proto-template for how he later analyzes his own family in the 2024 dad note and the Yahir family entity itself.

Connections

  • uo · the other wes moore · ecological model
  • school to prison pipeline · redlining · scholarships · job corps · johns hopkins
  • first generation guilt · college admissions
  • 2023 05 05 happy birthday poem · 2021 04 19 why osu · yahir family
